elements.sent-2411-001: Using first-principles calculations, we systematically investigate the spin contributions to the inverse Faraday effect (IFE) in transition metals.
elements.sent-2411-002: The IFE is primarily driven by spin-orbit coupling (SOC)-induced asymmetry between excited electron and hole spin moments.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our results reveal that even elements with smaller electron magnetic moments, like Os, can exhibit higher IFE due to greater electron-hole asymmetry.
elements.sent-2411-004: Pt shows the highest IFE in the 1$–$2~eV frequency range, while Os dominates in the 2$–$4~eV range.
elements.sent-2411-005: In addition, we demonstrate that the IFE of neighboring elements with similar crystal structures (e.g., Ir, Pt, and Au) can be tuned by adjusting their Fermi levels, indicating the importance of $d$ electron filling on IFE.
elements.sent-2411-006: Finally, we find that the trend in electron (or hole) contributions to the IFE closely follows that of the spin Hall conductivity (SHC), however, the total IFE involves more complex interactions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Introduction The inverse Faraday effect (IFE) is a nonlinear optical phenomenon where circularly polarized light (CPL) induces magnetic moments in a material without requiring an external magnetic field~\cite{van1965,Pershan1966 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: This ability enables all-optical helicity-dependent switching (AO-HDS)~\cite{Mangin_2012,Lambert2014,Mangin2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Experimentally, IFE has been shown to reverse the magnetization of ferrimagnets with a strong laser pulse~\cite{Kimel2005,Stanciu2007 and allows laser light to exert torques on magnetic metals~\cite{Choi2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Given these capabilities, IFE holds significant potential for applications in ultrafast magnetism~\cite{RevModPhys2010_Rasing and magneto-optics~\cite{Cheng2020,Ortiz2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The IFE was first described phenomenologically in the 1960s~\cite{Pitaevskii1961,Pershan1963 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Later, it was revisited, and a model for IFE in an isotropic, collisionless electron plasma was derived~\cite{HERTEL2006L1 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: To better understand the behavior in real materials, several quantum mechanical theories of IFE have been proposed, particularly for magnetic metals~\cite{Berritta2016,Freimuth2016,Scheid2019,Scheid_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: For non-magnetic metals with inversion symmetry, special attention is needed due to the doubly degenerate nature of their electronic bands.
elements.sent-2411-015: In our previous work~\cite{Shashi2023 , we developed a gauge-invariant IFE theory that accounts for the induced spin contributions in such materials.}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this study, we examine the spin contributions to the IFE in 3$d$, 4$d$, and 5$d$ transition metals to understand how various material properties influence the IFE and identify the key parameters governing it.
elements.sent-2411-017: Since most elements in these series are non-magnetic, except for Fe, Co, and Ni, we consider non-magnetic structures for all elements to analyze trends in the IFE as a function of valence electrons.
elements.sent-2411-018: The IFE in magnetic systems, which involves contributions from intraband excited electron-hole moments, will be explored in future work.
elements.sent-2411-019: Recently, there has been growing interest in studying trends in the spin Hall effect (SHE) and orbital Hall effect (OHE) among transition metals~\cite{Tanaka2008, Du2014, Jo2018, Salemi2022, Go2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: As we move across the periodic table, the number of valence electrons increases.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, as we move down the table from 3$d$ to 4$d$ and 5$d$ elements, both the spin-orbit interaction and $d$-band width increase, even though the number of valence electrons remains constant.
elements.sent-2411-022: Previous studies have shown that the SHE increases with the number of valence electrons and becomes more pronounced in metals with stronger SOC, whereas the OHE exhibits more complex behavior.
elements.sent-2411-023: To our knowledge, no prior studies have systematically explored how the IFE varies with the number of valence electrons.
elements.sent-2411-024: We analyzed the IFE for 30 transition metals using the expression outlined in Eq.~\eqref{eq:ife_all_three , which accounts for contributions from both excited electron and hole moments.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Interestingly, we found that the trend in electron moments closely aligns with that of the SHC, where a transverse electric field generates a spin current~\cite{Tanaka2008, Sinova2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, the total IFE is governed by more intricate factors, particularly the subtle asymmetries between the magnetic moments of excited electrons and holes.
elements.sent-2411-027: These asymmetries are influenced by spin-orbit interaction and the electronic structure of the materials.
elements.sent-2411-028: This mechanism is conceptually similar to the anomalous Hall effect (AHE) in ferromagnets, where an imbalance in the magnetic moments of conduction electrons, driven by SOC, produces a transverse voltage in response to an electric field~\cite{Karplus1954, Tanaka2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In the case of IFE, the SOC-driven asymmetry between excited electron and hole moments determines both the magnitude and sign of the effect.
elements.sent-2411-030: Among the metals studied, Pt exhibits the highest spin IFE in the 1$–$2~eV frequency range, while Os shows the largest IFE in the 2$–$4~eV range but with an opposite sign to that of Pt.
elements.sent-2411-031: We also demonstrated that the IFE values of Ir and Au can be made analogous with that of Pt by adjusting their Fermi levels.
elements.sent-2411-032: Such tuning of the Fermi level can be achieved through practical methods such as metal doping or alloying, offering a promising way to manipulate the IFE in these materials.

elements.sent-2411-037: For instance, in nonmagnetic metals with inversion symmetry, the bands are doubly degenerate~\cite{Elliott1954 , corresponding to spin-up and spin-down states in the absence of SOC.}

elements.sent-2411-057: Computational Methods We performed density functional theory calculations using Quantum { ESPRESSO ~\cite{Giannozzi2017 within the generalized gradient approximation~\cite{Perdew_1996 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: We used fully relativistic ONCV pseudopotentials~\cite{Hamann2013 from the pseudo-dojo library~\cite{VANSETTEN201839 .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The kinetic energy cutoff for the plane wave basis expansion was set to 120~Ry.
elements.sent-2411-060: We used experimental lattice parameters for all metals.
elements.sent-2411-061: We considered body-centered cubic (bcc) structures for V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Nb, Mo, Ta, and W, and face-centered cubic (fcc) structures for Ni, Cu, Rh, Pd, Ag, Ir, Pt, and Au.
elements.sent-2411-062: The bcc and fcc structures contain one atom in the primitive lattice.
elements.sent-2411-063: We considered hexagonal-close-packed (hcp) structures for Sc, Ti, Co, Y, Zr, Tc, Ru, Lu, Hf, Re, Os, Zn, and Cd, which have two atoms in the primitive cell.
elements.sent-2411-064: For Hg, we used the solid rhombohedral structure, which had two atoms in the primitive cell.
elements.sent-2411-065: We performed self-consistent calculations using k-meshes of $24\times24\times24$ for fcc and bcc structures, and $16\times16\times16$ for hcp metals.
elements.sent-2411-066: Non-self-consistent calculations were performed using $8\times8\times8$ k-grid.
elements.sent-2411-067: We constructed 18 atom-centered Wannier orbitals $sp^3d^2$, $d_{xy}$, $d_{yz}$, and $d_{zx}$ for fcc and bcc metals, and 36 Wannier functions for hcp metals, using Wannier90~\cite{Pizzi2020, Marzari2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-068: We used the Wannier~Berri~\cite{Stepan2021 package for Wannier interpolation~\cite{Wang2006 .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: We found that a k-point interpolation grid of $100\times100\times100$ is sufficient to obtain the convergence for fcc and bcc metals, while for hcp structures, we used k-meshes of $70\times70\times70$.
elements.sent-2411-070: To avoid singular points in the Brillouin zone with high symmetry, we shifted the uniform interpolation grid by a small random displacement along all three cartesian directions.
elements.sent-2411-071: We used a constant inverse lifetime($\eta$) of 0.1~eV in our calculations.
elements.sent-2411-072: We calculated the spin hall conductivity (SHC) for these metals with electric fields in the $x-y$ plane so that the spin polarizations are along the $z$ direction.
elements.sent-2411-073: We performed SHC calculations at $\omega$ = 0 with $\eta$ = 0.1~eV using Wannier~Berri~\cite{Qiao2018,Stepan2021 .}

elements.sent-2411-082: In each regime, we calculate the average IFE, with the results shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:mean-ife-total .}

elements.sent-2411-090: Based on the IFE trends, we classify these 30 elements into four groups.

elements.sent-2411-098: Group 10 elements, such as Ni, Pd, and Pt, exhibit higher positive IFE in the 1$-$2~eV range, with Pt showing the highest spin IFE among all metals in this range due to its nearly filled $d$ states and strong SOC.

elements.sent-2411-172: Summary In summary, we investigated the spin contributions to the IFE in 3$d$, 4$d$, and 5$d$ transition metals.
elements.sent-2411-173: The IFE depends on the asymmetry in excited electron and hole spin magnetic moments, which arises from differences in their band structures and the effects of spin-orbit coupling.
elements.sent-2411-174: Greater asymmetry leads to higher IFE values.
elements.sent-2411-175: We found that the trend in doubly-resonant electron IFE ($M^{\rm IFE _{\rm elec}$) with valence electrons aligns with the trend of spin Hall conductivity (SHC).}
elements.sent-2411-176: Like SHC, the sign of $M^{\rm IFE _{\rm elec}$ depends on the number of valence electrons.}
elements.sent-2411-177: However, the total spin IFE, which includes subtle asymmetries and non-doubly-resonant terms, does not correlate directly with SHC or JDOS.
elements.sent-2411-178: The SHC, IFE, and its decomposition terms are summarized in Table~\ref{tab:summary .}
elements.sent-2411-179: Our results highlight Pt as having the highest IFE in the 1$-$2~eV frequency range, while Os exhibits the highest IFE in the 2$-$4~eV range with the opposite sign to Pt.
elements.sent-2411-180: We also showed that the IFEs of neighboring elements with similar crystal structures can be aligned by adjusting their Fermi levels.
elements.sent-2411-181: This study provides insight into the variation of spin contributions to the IFE as a function of valence electrons and offers a deeper understanding of the underlying physics of spin IFE.
elements.sent-2411-182: While our current focus is on the spin component of IFE, we anticipate that the orbital component~\cite{Berritta2016,Go2024 will also play a significant role in future research.}

elements.sent-2411-201: Introduction The inverse Faraday effect (IFE) is a nonlinear optical phenomenon wherein circularly polarized light (CPL) induces a magnetic moment in a material without requiring an external magnetic field~\cite{van1965,Pershan1966 .}
elements.sent-2411-202: This opto-magnetic coupling enables all-optical helicity-dependent switching (AO-HDS)~\cite{Mangin_2012,Lambert2014,Mangin2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-203: Experimentally, IFE has been demonstrated to reverse the magnetization in ferrimagnets with a strong laser pulse~\cite{Kimel2005,Stanciu2007 , and to generate light-induced torques on magnetic metals~\cite{Choi2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-204: Given these capabilities, IFE holds a key mechanism for applications in ultrafast magnetism~\cite{RevModPhys2010_Rasing and magneto-optics~\cite{Cheng2020,Ortiz2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-205: However, for non-magnetic metals with inversion symmetry, where the electronic bands are doubly degenerate, special attention is needed.
elements.sent-2411-206: In our previous work~\cite{Shashi2023 , we developed a gauge-invariant IFE theory that accounts for the induced spin contributions in such systems.}
elements.sent-2411-207: In this study, we extend this framework to investigate the spin contributions to the IFE across 3$d$, 4$d$, and 5$d$ transition metals.
elements.sent-2411-208: Since most of these elements are non-magnetic, except Fe, Co, and Ni, we consider hypothetical non-magnetic configurations for these three elements to enable a consistent comparison across the series.
elements.sent-2411-209: This approach facilitates a systematic exploration of how the IFE evolves with the number of valence electrons and helps reveal the underlying material properties that govern the effect.
elements.sent-2411-210: The complex case of magnetic systems, where intraband contributions from photoexcited electron-hole pairs become significant, will be addressed in future work.
elements.sent-2411-211: Recent studies have highlighted trends in the spin Hall effect (SHE) across transition metals~\cite{Tanaka2008, Du2014, Jo2018, Salemi2022,Go2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-212: As one moves across a given row of the periodic table, the number of valence electrons increases.
elements.sent-2411-213: Conversely, moving down the table from 3$d$ to 5$d$ elements increases both spin-orbit interaction (SOI) strength and $d$-band width, even for elements with the same number of outer valence electrons.
elements.sent-2411-214: The SHE is known to scale with both the number of valence electrons and SOI strength.
elements.sent-2411-215: Despite these insights, a systematic study of how the IFE varies with valence electron count across transition metals remains lacking.
elements.sent-2411-216: To fill this gap, we analyzed the IFE in 30 transition metals using the theoretical expression given in Eq.~\eqref{eq:ife_all_three , which accounts for contributions from both excited electron and hole moments.}
elements.sent-2411-217: We find that the trend in doubly-resonant electron contributions to IFE closely aligns with that of the SHC, where a transverse electric field generates a spin current~\cite{Tanaka2008, Sinova2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-218: However, the total IFE depends on intricate factors, particularly the subtle asymmetries between the magnetic moments of excited electrons and holes.
elements.sent-2411-219: These asymmetries are influenced by SOI, differences in effective masses between electrons and holes~\cite{Fuseya2015 , and the intrinsic band structures of the materials~\cite{Karplus1954,Tanaka2008 .}}
elements.sent-2411-220: Among the systems studied, Pt exhibits the highest spin IFE in the 1$–$2~eV frequency range, while Os shows the strongest IFE response in the 2$–$4~eV range, but with an opposite sign to that of Pt.
elements.sent-2411-221: To elucidate the origin of Pt’s large IFE, we demonstrate that the IFE of its neighboring elements, Ir and Au, can be made comparable by tuning their Fermi levels.
elements.sent-2411-222: Such tuning—achievable via metal doping or alloying—offers a practical route to engineer the IFE response in transition metals.

elements.sent-2411-225: The main results are presented in Sec.~\ref{sec:results , followed by detailed discussion in Sec.~\ref{sec:discussion and summary in Sec.~\ref{sec:summary .}}}

elements.sent-2411-227: Theory The IFE is a second-order response of a material to an external electromagnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-228: A key consideration in this approach is the treatment of band degeneracy.
elements.sent-2411-229: For instance, in nonmagnetic metals with inversion symmetry, the bands are doubly degenerate~\cite{Elliott1954 , corresponding to spin-up and spin-down states in the absence of SOI.}
elements.sent-2411-230: With SOI, these states are still degenerate but cannot be labeled as pure spin states.
elements.sent-2411-231: The sum-over-states method poses challenges when dealing with such doubly degenerate bands~\cite{Pientka2012 , particularly in the second-order perturbation calculations of the IFE~\cite{Shashi2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-232: To systematically address this, we define each electronic state using a band index $n$ and a spinor-index $N$, where \(N=1,2\) distinguishes the two components of the degenerate doublet, corresponding to the same eigenenergy $E_{n\bm{k =E_{nN\bm{k }$, with $\bm k$ as the crystal momentum.}}}
elements.sent-2411-233: The IFE for such doubly degenerate bands can be decomposed into three distinct terms~\cite{Shashi2023 : \begin{equation}\label{eq:ife_all_three} M^{\rm IFE} = M^{\rm IFE}_{\rm elec}- M^{\rm IFE}_{\rm hole} + M^{\rm IFE}_{\rm ndr}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-234: Here, $M^{\rm IFE _{\rm elec}$ and $M^{\rm IFE _{\rm hole}$ represent doubly resonant contributions from excited electron and hole spin moments, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-235: These terms are expressed through the integrals over the first Brillouin zone (BZ) as follows: \begin{widetext \begin{equation}M^{\rm IFE}_{\rm elec} = \int_{\rm BZ} \frac{d^3k}{(2\pi)^3} \sum_n^{\rm occ} \sum_{N=1}^2 \sum_m^{\rm emp} \sum_{M=1}^2 \sum_{M'=1}^2 \frac{ \braket{\phi_{nNk} | V | \phi_{mMk}} \braket{\phi_{mMk} | M^{\rm spin} | \phi_{mM'k}} \braket{\phi_{mM'k} | V^{\dagger} | \phi_{nNk}} } {(E_{mk} - E_{nk} - \hbar \omega)^2 + \eta^2}, \label{eq:ife_elec} M^{\rm IFE}_{\rm hole} = \int_{\rm BZ} \frac{d^3k}{(2\pi)^3} \sum_n^{\rm occ} \sum_{N=1}^2 \sum_{N'=1}^2 \sum_m^{\rm emp} \sum_{M=1}^2 \frac{ \braket{\phi_{nNk} | V | \phi_{mMk}} \braket{\phi_{mMk} | V^{\dagger} | \phi_{nN'k}} \braket{\phi_{nN'k} | M^{\rm spin} | \phi_{nNk}} } {(E_{mk} - E_{nk} - \hbar \omega)^2 + \eta^2}. \label{eq:ife_hole}\end{equation} \end{widetext The electric field of the incident light is assumed to be in the $x$-$y$ plane, inducing the magnetic moment along the $z$-direction.}}

elements.sent-2411-242: If this second transition occurs within the spinor doublets, where the states are nearly degenerate in energy, the excitation and de-excitation levels are energetically matched, leading to a double-resonance condition.

elements.sent-2411-375: Summary We investigated the spin contributions to the IFE in 3$d$, 4$d$, and 5$d$ transition metals.
elements.sent-2411-376: The IFE originates from asymmetries between the spin magnetic moments of photoexcited electrons and holes, driven by differences in their band structures and the influence of SOI.
elements.sent-2411-377: Greater asymmetry leads to stronger IFE responses.
elements.sent-2411-378: We observed that doubly-resonant electron contributions to the IFE ($M^{\rm IFE _{\rm elec}$) follow a trend similar to the SHC, with both sign and magnitude depending on the number of valence electrons.}
elements.sent-2411-379: However, the total spin IFE, which includes subtle asymmetries and non-doubly-resonant terms, does not directly correlate with SHC or the JDOS.
elements.sent-2411-380: A full summary of SHC, total IFE, and its components is provided in Table~\ref{tab:summary .}
elements.sent-2411-381: Among all elements, Pt shows the highest spin IFE in the 1$-$2~eV range, while Os leads in the 2$-$4~eV range.
elements.sent-2411-382: We also demonstrated elements with similar crystal structures exhibit comparable IFE responses when their Fermi levels are aligned.
elements.sent-2411-383: These results highlight the role of valence electron filling and band structure in shaping the spin IFE and offer a foundation for future studies.

elements.sent-2411-388: The results show that the spin IFE magnitude remains unchanged with this denser \(\mathbf{k}\)-meshes, indicating convergence with respect to the NSCF grids.

elements.sent-2411-392: Therefore, for consistency, we employed the denser SCF \(\mathbf{k}\)-meshes of \(24 \times 24 \times 24\) across all fcc and bcc structures, throughout our study.
elements.figure-2411-031: \includegraphics[scale=0.9]{fig14-const-ife.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:cont-IFE} Effect of energy resonance and spin matrix elements on the IFE response in Pt and Os. Shown are the total IFE (\(M^{\rm IFE}\)), the doubly-resonant electron-hole contribution (\(M^{\rm IFE}_{\rm elec} - M^{\rm IFE}_{\rm hole} \equiv M_{\rm dr}^{\rm IFE}\)), and the non-doubly-resonant term (\(M_{\rm ndr}^{\rm IFE}\)). The terms \(M_{\rm const.\,\Delta E,\,dr}^{\rm IFE}\) and \(M_{\rm const.\,\Delta E,\,ndr}^{\rm IFE}\) represent the IFE contributions with the energy denominators set to unity in the doubly- and non-doubly-resonant terms, respectively. Similarly, \(M_{\rm const.\,M^{\rm spin},\,dr}^{\rm IFE}\) and \(M_{\rm const.\,M^{\rm spin},\,ndr}^{\rm IFE}\) are obtained by replacing the spin matrix elements with a constant value (\(\braket{\phi_{n\bm{k}} | M^{\rm spin} | \phi_{m\bm{k}}} = 1\)) in the respective terms.}
elements.figure-2411-032: \includegraphics[scale=0.97]{fig15-tc-ife.pdf} \caption{\label{fig:tc-ef-vary} Band structures and IFE response in hcp-Tc and hcp-Ru. In panel (a), the red dashed line indicates the Fermi level of Tc shifted upward by 1.5 eV. Panels (c)–(e) display the calculated IFE and its components for: (c) Tc at its actual \(E_{\rm F}\), (d) Tc with \(E_{\rm F}\) shifted by 1.5 eV, and (e) Ru at its actual \(E_{\rm F}\).}

elements.sent-2411-394: In 3$d$ series, for ferromagnetic Fe, Co, and Ni, hypothetical non-magnetic structures were used.
elements.sent-2411-395: Contributions from doubly-resonant and non-doubly-resonant terms nearly cancel for Zn resulting in negligible IFE.
elements.sent-2411-396: In some cases, such as fcc Ni and Cu, the non-doubly-resonant term is comparable in magnitude to the doubly-resonant term.
elements.sent-2411-397: Cu, in particular, shows an enhanced response in the 3$-$4 eV range due to $d \rightarrow s$ interband transitions.
elements.sent-2411-398: Early 3$d$ elements like Sc and Ti exhibit negligible $M^{\rm IFE}$ due to low $d$ electron density.
elements.sent-2411-399: Cr, with half-filled $d$ orbitals, shows large values of both $M^{\rm IFE _{\rm elec}$ and $M^{\rm IFE _{\rm hole}$, resulting in low asymmetry and total IFE.}}
elements.sent-2411-400: In 4$d$ metals, IFE increases with valence electrons up to Pd (with nearly filled $d$ and $s$ orbitals), then decreases for Ag and Cd.
elements.sent-2411-401: In 5$d$ elements, the non-doubly-resonant term is typically smaller, resulting in higher overall IFE values due to stronger SOI.
elements.sent-2411-402: Pt shows the highest spin IFE, followed by Ir in the 1$-$2~eV range, while Os exhibits the largest IFE response in the 2$-$4~eV range.
elements.sent-2411-403: Fully-filled systems, such as Hg, show negligible IFE, consistent with filled-shell behavior in Zn (3$d$) and Cd (4$d$).
elements.sent-2411-404: Analysis of doubly-resonant and non-doubly-resonant terms to IFE Figure~\ref{fig:cont-IFE compares the IFE response when energy resonance effects are suppressed (constant energy approximation, \(E_m - E_n \pm \hbar \omega + i\eta = 1\)) and when spin matrix elements are replaced with a constant value (\(\braket{\phi_{n{\bm k | M^{\rm spin | \phi_{m{\bm k = 1\)).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-405: The results show that setting constant energy preserves the relative dominance of doubly-resonant and non-doubly-resonant terms, confirming that energy resonance is the primary driver of the IFE.
elements.sent-2411-406: In contrast, replacing spin matrix elements with a constant value significantly reduces the overall IFE response, demonstrating that spin matrix elements alone contribute minimally.
elements.sent-2411-407: These findings highlight that while spin-orbit interaction (SOI) influences matrix elements, the dominant mechanism governing the IFE response in 5\(d\) elements is the energy resonance condition.
elements.sent-2411-408: IFE in hcp metals by Fermi level tuning For hcp crystals, we demonstrate the equivalence of IFE between Tc and Ru, by rigidly shifting the Fermi levels of Ru by 1.5~eV as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:tc-ef-vary .}
elements.sent-2411-409: It shows that the IFE of Tc is similar to that of Ru with the shifted Fermi level.
